title: Addressing food security in African cities

data_scope.comparative_cities: Harare (Zimbabwe), Lusaka (Zambia), Nairobi (Kenya), Kenyan small towns

core_conclusion: Urban food insecurity in sub-Saharan Africa affects over 50% of urban populations in least-developed countries, exceeding rural rates. Informal food traders — not supermarkets — are the primary affordable food source for poor households in African cities. Current urban planning visions systematically displace informal markets, eroding the food security and livelihoods of the urban poor. Effective policy must plan for the entire food system, including the symbiosis between formal and informal retail.
why_it_matters: Reframes food insecurity as an urban (not just rural) challenge; documents the irreplaceable role of informal traders using primary survey evidence; identifies a planning-market mismatch harming food-insecure households; links food systems to poverty, NCD burden, unemployment, and climate change; provides actionable recommendations for revised urban planning regulations.
why_nature: Multi-domain sustainability argument spanning food, poverty, health, climate, and urban governance; Global South perspective on a globally urgent challenge; challenges dominant supermarket-led planning paradigm with empirical evidence; directly actionable for SDGs 1, 2, and 11.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatial accessibility surface for informal food retail via 2SFCA/R2SFCA with mobility-calibrated catchments
Facility-location simulation of informal market displacement effects — efficiency-vs-equity trade-off for food access
Spatiotemporal retail-environment exposure index as GeoAI feature for diet-related NCD risk (MGWR/SHAP)
Informal-formal food supply network modeled as bipartite graph for shock propagation and resilience analysis
Geospatial foundation model fine-tuned on African urban morphology to produce an open food infrastructure quality index

related.title: Mapping urban slums and their inequality in sub-Saharan Africa
related.relation: provides spatial and socioeconomic slum mapping framework for scaling up Kisumu survey findings across African cities

related.title: Hundreds of grocery outlets needed across the United States to achieve walkable cities
related.relation: quantifies grocery-access deficits via facility-location methods — the formal-retail analog of the paper's informal-retail argument

related.title: Diet changes due to urbanization in South Africa are linked to microbiome and metabolome signatures of Westernization and colorectal cancer
related.relation: empirically links supermarket-driven dietary transition (the paper's core concern) to measurable health outcomes via microbiome data

related.title: Infrastructure for regional food systems to feed cities at a time of deglobalization
related.relation: extends the food-supply network framing to regional scale, complementing the paper's informal-formal supply symbiosis observation

related.title: Infrastructure deficits and informal settlements in sub-Saharan Africa
related.relation: provides fine-grained infrastructure deficit mapping across sub-Saharan African informal settlements — contextualizing the food-access challenge the paper describes

related.title: African food system and biodiversity mainly affected by urbanization via dietary shifts
related.relation: demonstrates how African urbanization reshapes food systems and biodiversity via dietary shifts, extending the paper's food-system framing to ecological consequences

figures.caption: Ten most-frequently-used sources of food in Kisumu, Kenya (2016) — 840 sampled households; informal traders dominate household food acquisition.
Proportion of food purchased sourced directly from supermarkets, Kisumu (2016) — despite supermarket presence, households source most food from non-supermarket outlets.
